data-dictionary-010.quickRevisionNotes:
What: A secondary index is a non-primary-key index created in SE11 to accelerate SELECT statements that filter on commonly used non-key columns.

Used for:
- Removing full table scans on large tables (MARC, BSEG, VBAK)
- Improving report runtime from seconds to milliseconds
- Supporting specific WHERE patterns identified via ST05 trace

Key syntax / tables / Tcodes:
- SE11 -> table -> Indexes tab -> Create; activate; SE14 if conversion needed
- ST05 to verify INDEX RANGE SCAN; DBACOCKPIT for HANA explain plan
- DD12L (index header), DD17S (index fields); Z* namespace for custom indexes

Interview keyword: Z01 namespace, ST05 before/after proof.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do you use ST05 to diagnose a missing index?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: ST05 process: ST05 mein SQL trace activate karo, slow ABAP report run karo, trace stop karo. Trace results mein, slow SQL statement identify karo — badi tables par FULL TABLE SCAN operation dhundo. Key metrics: milliseconds mein duration, rows examined versus rows returned. 100,000 examined to 10 returned ka ratio missing index signal hai. Trace se table name aur WHERE clause fields note karo. SE11 mein un fields par secondary index create karo. Improvement confirm karne ke liye trace re-run karo — rows examined ab approximately rows returned ke barabar hone chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: ST05 process: activate the SQL trace in ST05, run the slow ABAP report, stop the trace. In trace results, identify the slow SQL statement — look for FULL TABLE SCAN operation on large tables. Key metrics: duration in milliseconds, rows examined versus rows returned. A ratio of 100,000 examined to 10 returned signals a missing index. Note the table name and WHERE clause fields from the trace. Create a secondary index in SE11 on those fields. Re-run the trace to confirm improvement — rows examined should now match rows returned approximately.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Performance fix: Goods movement report was taking 8 minutes. ST05 trace showed SELECT on MSEG with WHERE WERKS + LGORT taking 450 seconds, 8 million rows scanned, 200 returned. Created secondary index MSEG~Z01 on WERKS + LGORT. Re-ran ST05 — same SELECT now 2 seconds, 200 rows scanned. Report time: 8 minutes to 45 seconds.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: ST05 use kiye bina 'index add karo' recommend karna. ST05 se confirm karo ki actually full table scan hai — sometimes slow report ka reason index nahi, network ya application logic hoti hai. Diagnose first, then fix.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" ST05 SQL Trace Steps:
" 1. Transaction ST05 -> Activate Trace
" 2. Run slow ABAP report in same session
" 3. ST05 -> Deactivate Trace -> Display Trace
" 4. Look for: REOPEN/FETCH with large row count
" 5. Note: Table name, WHERE fields, duration

" Trace output example (conceptual):
" Table: MSEG | Duration: 450000ms | Rows: 8000000
" Operation: FULL TABLE SCAN
" WHERE: WERKS = '1000' AND LGORT = 'WH01'

" Action: SE11 -> MSEG -> Indexes -> MSEG~Z01
" Fields: WERKS, LGORT -> Activate

followupAnswerBank.question: What fields should you NOT create a secondary index on?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Secondary indexes inn par create mat karo: low cardinality fields jahan sirf few distinct values exist karte hain — database optimizer index ignore karega aur full scan kar dega. Few thousand rows se kam wali chhoti tables — full scan pehle se kafi fast hai aur index overhead unnecessary hai. Bahut frequently updated fields — har INSERT aur UPDATE par uss table ke saare indexes update karne padte hain, write latency badhati hai. Primary key ka part wale fields — pehle se indexed hain. Rarely WHERE clauses mein appear karne wale fields — index maintenance cost read benefit se zyada hai. Over-indexing ek real anti-pattern hai jo write performance hurt karti hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Do not create secondary indexes on: low cardinality fields where only a few distinct values exist — the database optimizer will ignore the index and do a full scan anyway. Small tables with under a few thousand rows — full scan is already fast enough and index overhead is unnecessary. Very frequently updated fields — every INSERT and UPDATE must also update all indexes on that table, increasing write latency. Fields that are part of the primary key — already indexed. Fields rarely appearing in WHERE clauses — the index maintenance cost exceeds any read benefit. Over-indexing is a real anti-pattern that hurts write performance.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Wrong index scenario: Developer added index on VBAK-VKORG (sales organization) field because a report filtered by it. Cardinality: only 3 values in the whole system (sales orgs 1000, 2000, 3000). Database optimizer ignored the index completely — still doing full scan. Index existed with zero benefit but added overhead to every VBAK insert. Removed the index — no performance change for the report, but write performance improved slightly.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Har WHERE clause field pe index banana. Low cardinality check karo pehle — COUNT(DISTINCT field) / COUNT(*) ratio. Agar ratio 0.01 se kam hai — index probably not useful. Database optimizer cardinality statistics use karta hai index decision ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Check cardinality before creating index
SELECT COUNT( DISTINCT vkorg ) AS dist_vals,
       COUNT( * )               AS total_rows
  FROM vbak
  INTO @DATA(ls_card).
DATA lv_ratio TYPE p LENGTH 8 DECIMALS 4.
lv_ratio = ls_card-dist_vals * '1.0' / ls_card-total_rows.
" Cast to decimal — integer division would always give 0 for ratio < 1
" If lv_ratio < 0.01 -> low cardinality -> skip index
" If lv_ratio > 0.1  -> high cardinality -> index beneficial

" High cardinality (good for index): MATNR, KUNNR, LIFNR
" Low cardinality (avoid index): VKORG, MTART, SHKZG

followupAnswerBank.question: How does a secondary index affect INSERT and UPDATE performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Har secondary index write operations mein maintenance overhead add karta hai. Table mein har INSERT uss table par saare active indexes update karta hai. Indexed field ka har UPDATE corresponding index bhi update karta hai. VBAK ya BKPF jaisi high-volume transaction tables jahan per hour thousands of records insert hote hain, excessive secondary indexes noticeably insert throughput slow karte hain. Rule: ST05 ke through identified proven performance issues ke liye hi secondary indexes create karo, preemptively nahi. Mass INSERT wale batch programs mein, temporarily indexes disable karna, data load karna, aur indexes rebuild karna more efficient ho sakta hai — though yeh careful planning require karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Every secondary index adds maintenance overhead to write operations. Each INSERT into a table updates all active indexes on that table. Each UPDATE of an indexed field also updates the corresponding index. In high-volume transaction tables like VBAK or BKPF where thousands of records are inserted per hour, excessive secondary indexes noticeably slow down insert throughput. The rule: create secondary indexes only for proven performance issues identified via ST05, not preemptively. In batch programs with mass INSERT, temporarily disabling indexes, loading data, and rebuilding indexes can be more efficient — though this requires careful planning.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Batch program impact: Mass goods receipt program inserting 100,000 MSEG rows per night. After adding three secondary indexes on MSEG for reporting, batch runtime increased from 2 hours to 4 hours. Root cause: index maintenance for 100,000 × 3 indexes. Solution: analyzed which index was really needed — kept one critical index, removed two. Batch time: 2.5 hours — acceptable compromise.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Sirf read performance ke bare mein sochna aur indexes freely add karna. Write-heavy tables pe extra indexes add karne se batch job performance seriously impact hoti hai. Read vs write trade-off hamesha evaluate karo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Index maintenance overhead (conceptual):
" Table VBAK with 3 secondary indexes:
" INSERT INTO VBAK -> also updates index Z01, Z02, Z03
" 3x extra write operations per INSERT

" Monitor index usage (HANA-specific view):
SELECT schema_name, table_name, index_name, read_count, write_count
  FROM m_cs_indexes
  WHERE schema_name = 'SAPHANADB'
    AND table_name = 'VBAK'.
" read_count low + write_count high -> consider removing index

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the naming convention for customer secondary indexes in SE11?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Customer secondary indexes ko SE11 mein customer namespace prefix use karna padta hai. Format hai: TABLE_NAME~ZXX jahan Z customer namespace indicate karta hai aur XX two-character alphanumeric identifier hai — for example MARC~Z01, VBAK~Z01. SAP-delivered indexes numeric suffixes use karte hain jaise MARA~0, MARA~1. Tilde character table name ko index ID se separate karta hai. Yeh naming convention ensure karta hai ki SAP upgrades customer indexes overwrite na karein. Same table par multiple customer indexes MARC~Z01, MARC~Z02 aise named hote hain. Hamesha Z prefix use karo — kabhi bhi customer indexes namespace prefix ke bina mat create karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Customer secondary indexes must use the customer namespace prefix in SE11. The format is: TABLE_NAME~ZXX where Z indicates customer namespace and XX is a two-character alphanumeric identifier — for example MARC~Z01, VBAK~Z01. SAP-delivered indexes use numeric suffixes like MARA~0, MARA~1. The tilde character separates table name from index ID. This naming convention ensures SAP upgrades do not overwrite customer indexes. Multiple customer indexes on the same table are named MARC~Z01, MARC~Z02, and so on. Always use Z prefix — never create customer indexes without namespace prefix.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Naming issue: New developer created index MARC~01 (without Z prefix). During SAP patch application, SAP's own index MARC~01 conflicted. Transport error in QA. Root cause: wrong namespace. Fixed by deleting MARC~01 and creating MARC~Z01 with same fields. Lesson: Z prefix is not optional.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Index banate waqt Z prefix bhool jana. MARC~01 create karna — SAP namespace conflict risk. Always MARC~Z01 format use karo. SE11 mein index creation form mein index ID field mein 'Z01' daalo.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" SE11: Create Secondary Index
" 1. SE11 -> Enter table name: MARC -> Display
" 2. Goto -> Indexes -> Create Index
" 3. Index Name field: Z01  (system adds table~)
"    Full index name becomes: MARC~Z01
" 4. Add fields: WERKS, MATNR
" 5. Select: Non-unique index
" 6. Activate

" Verify index exists:
SELECT indexname, dbindex, tabname
  FROM dd17s
  WHERE tabname = 'MARC'
    AND indexname LIKE 'Z%'.

followupAnswerBank.question: How do indexes work differently in SAP HANA versus classic databases?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: HANA ka column store row-based databases se fundamentally different hai: data column by column store hota hai, toh specific columns select karne wali queries sirf relevant column data read karti hain — massive I/O reduction. Bahut sari queries jo row-based databases par indexes need karti thi HANA par column compression aur vectorized processing ke karan efficiently bina indexes run hoti hain. However, SE11 secondary indexes badi tables par high-cardinality equality lookups aur range queries ke liye HANA par bhi benefit dete hain. HANA apni statistics bhi maintain karta hai aur index skip scans use kar sakta hai. Approach: yeh mat assume karo ki HANA par indexes irrelevant hain. ST05 ya HANA Studio mein HANA SQL trace use karo confirm karne ke liye ki index aapke specific query pattern ke liye help karta hai.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: HANA's column store fundamentally differs from row-based databases: data is stored column by column, so queries selecting specific columns only read relevant column data — massive I/O reduction. Many queries that needed indexes on row-based databases run efficiently on HANA without them due to column compression and vectorized processing. However, SE11 secondary indexes still provide benefit on HANA for high-cardinality equality lookups and range queries on large tables. HANA also maintains its own statistics and can use index skip scans. The approach: do not assume indexes are irrelevant on HANA. Use ST05 or HANA SQL trace in HANA Studio to confirm whether an index helps your specific query pattern.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: S/4HANA performance test: Report on MARC with WHERE WERKS + BWKEY ran in 3 seconds without index on HANA — acceptable. Same report on ECC with DB2 without index: 45 seconds — unacceptable. Added index for ECC. On S/4HANA: tested with index — 1.8 seconds. Marginal improvement but confirmed via HANA SQL trace. Kept index on S/4HANA too for consistent performance under peak load.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: S/4HANA pe migrate hone ke baad 'HANA hai to index zaroorat nahi' assume karna aur sab custom indexes drop karna. Test first with ST05 / HANA trace. Some indexes still provide meaningful benefit especially for very large tables with specific access patterns.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" HANA SQL Trace (in HANA Studio / DBA Cockpit):
" DB02 -> SQL Cache -> Find slow queries
" Or: DBACOCKPIT -> Diagnostics -> SQL Monitor

" ABAP: ST05 works same way on HANA
" Trace shows HANA execution plan: INDEX SEARCH vs FULL SCAN

" HANA-specific: check if index is being used
SELECT schema_name, table_name, column_name,
       distinct_count, record_count
  FROM m_cs_columns
  WHERE table_name = 'MARC'
    AND column_name IN ( 'WERKS', 'MATNR' ).
" High distinct_count ratio -> good candidate for index

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the difference between a unique and a non-unique secondary index?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Unique secondary index enforce karta hai ki indexed fields ka combination saare rows mein unique ho — duplicate combination insert karne par database error aati hai. Non-unique secondary index sirf performance purposes serve karta hai — indexed fields mein duplicate values allowed hain. ABAP development mein, secondary indexes almost always non-unique hote hain kyunki hum non-key fields index karte hain jahan duplicates naturally hote hain. Unique indexes tab sense karte hain jab business rule non-primary-key fields par uniqueness require karta hai — for example, ZORDER table ka EXTERNAL_ORDER_NUMBER par unique index ho sakta hai duplicate external references prevent karne ke liye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: A unique secondary index enforces that the combination of indexed fields is unique across all rows — attempting to insert a duplicate combination causes a database error. A non-unique secondary index only serves performance purposes — duplicate values in indexed fields are allowed. In ABAP development, secondary indexes are almost always non-unique because we index non-key fields where duplicates naturally occur. Unique indexes make sense only when a business rule requires uniqueness on non-primary-key fields — for example, a ZORDER table might have a unique index on EXTERNAL_ORDER_NUMBER to prevent duplicate external references.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Unique index use case: Custom table ZEXTERNAL_ORDERS had field ZEXT_ORDER_NO (external system order ID). Business rule: no two rows can have same external order number. Created a unique secondary index on ZEXT_ORDER_NO. When import program tried to insert duplicate, DB returned unique constraint error — caught in ABAP exception handling and reported as duplicate prevention.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Performance ke liye unique index banana performance-only fields pe. Unique index extra constraint check karta hai har INSERT pe — slight overhead. Use unique index only when uniqueness is a real business requirement, not just for performance.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Non-unique index (most common - for performance)
" SE11 -> MARC -> Indexes -> Create
" Index: MARC~Z01, Fields: WERKS
" Unique: unchecked  <- non-unique

" Unique index (for business rule enforcement)
" SE11 -> ZEXTERNAL_ORDERS -> Indexes -> Create
" Index: ZEXTERNAL_ORDERS~Z01, Fields: ZEXT_ORDER_NO
" Unique: checked  <- enforces uniqueness

" ABAP: Handle unique constraint violation
INSERT INTO zexternal_orders VALUES ls_order.
IF sy-subrc = 4.  " Duplicate key
  MESSAGE 'External order number already exists' TYPE 'E'.
ENDIF.

followupAnswerBank.question: How does the order of fields in a secondary index affect performance?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Secondary index ka leading field critical hota hai — database optimizer index use kar sakta hai sirf jab leading field WHERE clause mein appear kare. Agar index WERKS + MATNR ke roop mein defined hai aur WHERE clause sirf MATNR par filter karta hai (WERKS par nahi), index use nahi hoga — full table scan result hota hai. Field order strategy: most frequently used WHERE clause field pehle place karo. Agar dono fields hamesha WHERE mein together appear karte hain, more selective (higher cardinality) field pehle place karo better index efficiency ke liye. Field order decide karne se pehle ST05 traces ke through actual WHERE clause usage patterns analyze karo.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: The leading field of a secondary index is critical — the database optimizer can use the index only when the leading field appears in the WHERE clause. If the index is defined as WERKS + MATNR and your WHERE clause filters only on MATNR (not WERKS), the index will not be used — resulting in a full table scan. Field order strategy: place the most frequently used WHERE clause field first. If both fields always appear together in WHERE, place the more selective (higher cardinality) field first for better index efficiency. Analyze actual WHERE clause usage patterns via ST05 traces before deciding field order.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Index field order mistake: Index on MARC created as BWTAR + WERKS. Most queries filtered by WERKS only — BWTAR was the leading field not in WHERE clause. Index was never used. ST05 confirmed full scan despite index. Fixed: recreated index as WERKS + BWTAR. Leading field WERKS now matched WHERE clause — index used, query 20x faster.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: Index fields ko alphabetically ya randomly order karna. Leading field strategy is critical. Analyze WHERE clause patterns first — which field appears most, which is most selective — then order accordingly. Wrong field order = index exists but never used.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" WHERE clause pattern analysis:
" Query 1: WHERE werks = X AND matnr = Y  (90% of queries)
" Query 2: WHERE werks = X only           (10% of queries)
" Never: WHERE matnr = Y only

" CORRECT index field order: WERKS first (leading field)
" Index MARC~Z01: WERKS, MATNR
" Query 1: uses index (WERKS in WHERE) -> fast
" Query 2: uses index (WERKS only = index range scan) -> fast

" WRONG index field order: MATNR first
" Index MARC~Z02: MATNR, WERKS
" Query 2 (WHERE werks only): leading field MATNR missing -> FULL SCAN

followupAnswerBank.question: What is the process to verify index creation in SE11 and confirm it is active in the database?
followupAnswerBank.hinglishExplanation: Index creation verification ke do layers hain. Layer 1 — ABAP Dictionary: SE11 index definition Active ya Inactive status ke saath dikhata hai. Layer 2 — Database level: SE14 Database Utility confirm karta hai ki index physically database mein exist karta hai. Process: SE11 mein index create karo, activate karo — activation database CREATE INDEX statement trigger karta hai. SE14 mein, table select karo, database check karo — DB-level index existence confirm karta hai. Creation ke baad, ST05 use karke slow report run karo aur verify karo ki trace ab FULL TABLE SCAN ki jagah INDEX SEEK dikhata hai. Complete verification ke liye dono layers confirmed hone chahiye.
followupAnswerBank.interviewAnswer: Index creation verification has two layers. Layer 1 — ABAP Dictionary: SE11 shows the index definition with status Active or Inactive. Layer 2 — Database level: SE14 Database Utility confirms whether the index physically exists in the database. Process: create index in SE11, activate it — activation triggers database CREATE INDEX statement. In SE14, select the table, check database — confirms DB-level index existence. After creation, use ST05 to run the slow report again and verify the trace now shows INDEX SEEK instead of FULL TABLE SCAN. Both layers must be confirmed for complete verification.
followupAnswerBank.realtimeExplanation: Incomplete index scenario: Index created and activated in SE11 DEV. Transported to QA. SE11 in QA showed index active. But ST05 in QA still showed full scan. Root cause: DB adjustment failed during transport import — DDIC active but DB index not created. SE14 confirmed: index missing in database. Fix: SE14 → Activate and Adjust Database → index physically created in QA DB. ST05 then confirmed INDEX SEEK.
followupAnswerBank.mistakes: Galti: SE11 mein index Active dikhe to assume karna ki DB mein bhi hai. Transport ya activation failure se DDIC aur DB out of sync ho sakte hain. Post-transport: SE14 se DB level verify karo AND ST05 se query behavior confirm karo — dono steps important hain.
followupAnswerBank.codeExample:
" Verify index in ABAP Dictionary:
SELECT indexname, tabname, dbindex, sqltab
  FROM dd17s
  WHERE tabname = 'MARC'
    AND indexname = 'Z01'
    AND as4local = 'A'.
" as4state = 'A' -> Active in DDIC

" SE14: Database Utility
" SE14 -> Enter table: MARC -> Check/Activate
" Confirms: index exists in physical DB

" ST05 verification after index creation:
" Run report with trace -> check operation type
" Before index: REOPEN + FULL TABLE SCAN
" After index:  REOPEN + INDEX SEEK on MARC~Z01
